figures.caption: Figure 2: Body-fitted function transforms SDF into a geometric descriptor that can automatically satisfy the Dirichlet boundary condition.
Figure 3: The body-fitted function is compressed using C-HiDeNN Tucker Decomposition
Figure 5: SDF and BFF Φβ for the 2D circular domain (R = 0.7). The black solid line marks the domain boundary ∂Ω.
Figure 6: Convergence of the relative L2 error (left) and relative energy error (right) for the 2D circular domain (β = 0.01). Triangles indicate the theoretical optimal convergence rates.

Figure 7: Relative L2 error (left) and relative energy error (right) versus void stabilization parameter γ on a fixed 128 × 128 mesh (p = 3). The fitted slope over points 2-5 (γ = 10−3 to 10−6) is annotated on each panel. The red-shaded region indicates the Discretization-dominated regime (γ ≲ 10−7)., in which the total error saturates.
Figure 8: SDF and BFF Φβ for the 2D annular domain with eccentric hole (R1 = 0.7, R2 = 0.2, x0 = 0.15, β = 0.01). The black solid lines mark the outer boundary and the inner hole.

Figure 10: Solution fields for the 2D starfish domain with a central hole. Left: Body-fitted FEniCSx reference solution. Middle: Immersed CHiDeNN solution (uh = Φvh). Right: Point-wise absolute error between the immersed and reference solutions.

Figure 11: Solution fields for the 2D gear domain with a central hole. Left: Body-fitted FEniCSx reference solution. Middle: Immersed C-HiDeNN solution (uh = Φvh). Right: Point-wise absolute error between the immersed and reference solutions.

Figure 12: Convergence of the relative L2 error (left) and relative energy error (right) for the 3D sphere with a concentric spherical void (R1 = 0.7, R2 = 0.3, xc = (1, 1, 1)). The triangles indicate the fitted slopes.
